planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to partial shade; confederate jasmine is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions and thrives in both sun and partial shade. Train onto a trellis or fence early — without support it sprawls as a mounding groundcover.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; confederate jasmine (not a true jasmine but a related genus) is one of the most beloved and widely planted fragrant vines in Gulf Coast landscapes, producing masses of small, pinwheel-shaped, pure-white flowers with an intoxicating, sweet-spicy fragrance that perfumes an entire garden in spring bloom. Glossy, dark-green, evergreen leaves on twining stems provide year-round foliage interest. It is remarkably adaptable — trained on trellises and arbors as a vertical element, allowed to sprawl as a groundcover, used as a sprawling shrub, or pruned as a low hedge. In zone 8–11 it is fully evergreen and essentially indestructible once established, tolerating heat, drought, poor soil, and salt spray. Prune back after flowering to keep it tidy and encourage dense regrowth. One of the most reliable and rewarding plants in the Gulf Coast garden.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring or fall in any well-drained soil in full shade to full sun; asiatic jasmine is extremely adaptable and one of the easiest groundcovers to establish in the South. Space 12–18 inches apart and mulch heavily until the ground is covered — the canopy fills in relatively quickly in warm weather.

directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full shade to full sun; asiatic jasmine is the premier shade-tolerant groundcover for zone 7–11 landscapes — a dense, weed-suppressing mat of small, oval, glossy, dark-green leaves on wiry twining stems that fills in rapidly and maintains its deep color year-round. Unlike its fragrant relative confederate jasmine (T. jasminoides), asiatic jasmine rarely flowers in the landscape and is grown purely for its exceptional foliage ground cover performance. It is the go-to solution for the problem spots in Southern landscapes: dry shade under large oaks and magnolias, slopes that are difficult to mow, areas around tree roots where nothing else grows. Once established, it requires almost no maintenance — shear or mow to 3–4 inches in early spring every few years to remove the older, woody stems and stimulate fresh, dense regrowth. Remarkably drought tolerant, heat tolerant, and deer resistant. Variegated forms ('Ogon Nishiki') have yellow-green leaves that brighten shaded areas.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; African iris blooms most profusely in full sun but tolerates partial shade well. Avoid deadheading spent flower stalks — the branching stems continue to produce new flower buds from the same stalk for months.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; African iris is one of the most practical and reliable perennials for zone 8–11 landscapes, earning its common name 'fortnight lily' because it blooms in cycles roughly every two weeks throughout the warm season. The elegant white flowers with yellow and purple markings closely resemble true iris flowers but are produced on branching stalks that continue reblooming without deadheading — the spent blooms drop cleanly without the need for any grooming. The clumping, strap-like, evergreen leaves provide bold, attractive foliage year-round. It is exceptionally drought, heat, salt, and poor-soil tolerant once established, performing equally well in coastal and inland gardens. Divide congested clumps every 4–5 years in spring. A closely related species, Dietes bicolor (yellow African iris), produces pale yellow flowers with purple spots and is similarly adaptable. An outstanding low-maintenance perennial for mass plantings, borders, and water-wise landscapes.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; walking iris tolerates a range of light conditions but performs best and blooms most freely in bright filtered shade. In zone 9 it may be damaged by hard freezes but regrows vigorously from established rhizomes.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; walking iris (Neomarica gracilis) takes its name from its fascinating method of reproduction: after the short-lived fragrant iris-like flowers (white with blue and brown markings) appear at the tip of a fan-shaped leaf stalk, plantlets form at the same tip and the stalk bends under their weight until the plantlets touch the ground and root — 'walking' the plant outward from the mother clump. The tropical-looking, fan-shaped clumps of stiff, flat, strap-like leaves grow in dense stands that are ornamental year-round. The flowers appear several times through the warm season, each lasting only a day but produced in succession over weeks. Also called the 'apostle plant' because the original observation was that each stalk must produce 12 leaves (one for each apostle) before flowering — an observation that is approximately correct for mature stalks. An outstanding low-maintenance shade plant for zone 9–12 that naturalized easily in humid, subtropical gardens.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil or in a container with orchid-type epiphytic potting mix; blue star fern often grows as an epiphyte on the rough bark of cabbage palms and oak trees in the wild. It tolerates both wet and dry conditions remarkably well for a fern.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained to moderately dry soil in partial to full shade; blue star fern (golden polypody) is one of the most striking native ferns of the Gulf Coast and Florida, producing large, deeply lobed, silvery blue-green fronds with a distinctive blue-gray cast unusual in ferns — the fronds can reach 3 feet long and create a bold, sculptural effect in the shade garden. It is one of the few ferns that grows naturally as an epiphyte on the rough, furrowed bark of cabbage palms (Sabal palmetto) and live oaks in coastal Florida and the Gulf states, where it wedges into bark crevices and grows without soil, sustained by rainfall and humidity. In garden cultivation it is more drought tolerant than most ferns, making it valuable for drier shaded spots. The orange-yellow round sori on the frond undersides are an attractive feature. An outstanding large, architectural fern for zone 8–12 shade gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade; deer fern requires strongly acidic soil and consistent moisture — it declines in neutral or alkaline conditions. It is best suited to the Pacific Coast, Pacific Northwest, and mild Atlantic coastal gardens with cool summers.

directions: Plant in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade; deer fern is one of the most distinctive of all woodland ferns, producing two very different types of fronds simultaneously from the central crown: outer, spreading, dark-green sterile fronds that lie nearly flat to the ground, and inner, erect, narrowly pinnate fertile fronds that stand upright with narrow, blade-like pinnae bearing the spore-producing sori on their undersides. This dramatic dimorphism makes it immediately recognizable in the forest. It is a characteristic plant of the cool, moist, acidic coniferous and mixed forests of the Pacific Northwest (where it is extremely common) and also occurs in Atlantic coastal forests and in Europe, where it was traditionally fed to deer (hence the common name). It requires cool, humid conditions and consistently moist, strongly acidic soil to thrive — it is less suited to hot, dry interior gardens. An outstanding structural fern for the cool, shaded woodland garden.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; black mondo grass spreads slowly and is best spaced 6–8 inches apart for a groundcover effect — it takes 2–3 seasons to fill in. The near-black foliage color intensifies in partial sun; deep shade reduces color intensity.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; black mondo grass is one of the most dramatic and unique foliage plants available for shaded landscapes, producing very narrow, arching, strappy leaves that are such a dark purple-black that they appear nearly jet black — an almost unparalleled foliage color in horticulture. Small, pale pink flowers on short stalks appear in summer, followed by jet-black berries that provide additional ornamental interest through winter. It spreads slowly by underground rhizomes, forming low, slow-expanding clumps that work beautifully as a groundcover, edging plant, or textural accent in shade gardens. The black foliage creates extraordinary contrast with silver-leaved plants (Japanese painted fern, brunnera) or with the bright lime-green of golden creeping Jenny. It provides the most intense color in partial shade with some morning sun. Divide congested clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor and appearance.

planting.note: Direct sow seed or plant stem cuttings after the last frost in rich, moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; water spinach grows extremely rapidly in heat and humidity and prefers consistently moist to wet conditions — it can be grown in shallow water up to 6 inches deep. Note: cultivation is federally restricted in the US due to invasive potential — check local regulations before growing.

directions: Direct sow or root stem cuttings after frost in rich, moist to wet soil in full sun to partial shade; water spinach (kangkong in Filipino/Malaysian, rau muống in Vietnamese, ong choy in Cantonese) is one of the most important leafy green vegetables in Southeast, South, and East Asian cuisines, prized for its mild flavor, tender texture, and extremely rapid growth in warm, humid weather — it can be harvested in as little as 30 days from planting and can produce multiple harvests per season. The hollow stems and young leaf tips are stir-fried in garlic and oyster sauce in countless Southeast Asian dishes. It grows prolifically in the heat and humidity of zone 9–12 and is one of the most productive warm-season greens available. Important regulatory note: Ipomoea aquatica is classified as a federal noxious weed in the US and cultivation may require a state permit in some states — verify local regulations before growing. In Florida and other Gulf states it should be grown in contained beds well away from natural waterways.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Division

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil or attach to rough bark in partial to full shade; licorice fern is an epiphytic Pacific Coast fern that grows naturally on mossy boulders, tree trunks, and cliff faces — it requires excellent drainage but consistent moisture during its winter growing season and goes dormant in summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — most charming when allowed to grow on the surface of mossy boulders, log stumps, or the rough bark of large-trunked trees; licorice fern is a graceful, small, creeping fern of the cool, moist Pacific Coast woodlands, named for the sweet licorice-like flavor of its slender rhizomes, which were chewed fresh by Pacific Northwest indigenous peoples to sweeten foods and treat respiratory ailments. The fronds are bright, glossy green, simply pinnate, and arch gracefully; the plant is unusual in that it grows actively through the cool, wet winter months and goes completely dormant in summer when conditions become warm and dry. In gardens it performs best in mild coastal climates (Pacific Coast, mild Atlantic coast) with cool, moist winters and moderate summers. Established plants drape beautifully over mossy surfaces and create a lush, ancient, fern-grotto effect in shaded rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in fall or spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; western sword fern is exceptionally drought tolerant for a fern once established and can grow in drier conditions than most shade ferns. Best suited to mild coastal gardens and the Pacific Coast; tolerates moderate heat in zone 9 with adequate moisture.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; western sword fern is the dominant groundcover fern of the Pacific Coast forest — so abundant under the conifer canopy from Alaska to California that it defines the character of the Pacific Northwest woodland aesthetic. It produces large, upright vase-shaped crowns of long, lance-shaped, once-pinnate, deep-green fronds with distinctive little ear-like pinnae at the base of each pinnule. The fronds are evergreen and persistent, providing year-round cover and beauty. Unlike many ferns, western sword fern is remarkably drought tolerant once established, making it suitable for drier shaded conditions under conifers where moisture-loving ferns fail. The leathery fronds are long-lasting as cut foliage in arrangements. A superb structural fern for the cool, moist shade garden; it works at large scale as a groundcover under conifers, at woodland edges, and in large drift plantings where its bold, persistent fronds create a dependable year-round backdrop.
